name: nosql-expert
description: Expert guidance for distributed NoSQL databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB). Focuses on mental models, query-first modeling, single-table design, and avoiding hot partitions in high-scale systems.
capabilities: Explain query-first data modeling for distributed NoSQL databases
Guide on partition key design to prevent hot partitions
Provide single-table design patterns for DynamoDB
Explain Cassandra primary key structure and clustering columns
Help optimize read patterns and avoid scatter-gather queries
Compare SQL vs NoSQL mental models for data design
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is purely educational documentation about NoSQL database design patterns. The static analyzer detected patterns (external_commands, weak_crypto, system_reconnaissance) that are FALSE POSITIVES - they are documentation examples of database table names, query syntax, and design concepts. No actual code execution, network calls, or file operations exist.
valueStatement: This skill provides expert guidance for designing distributed NoSQL databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB). It helps developers understand query-first modeling, single-table design, and avoid common pitfalls like hot partitions.
limitations: This skill provides guidance only - it does not execute database commands
Does not include code implementations for specific databases
Does not connect to actual Cassandra or DynamoDB instances
Cannot generate production-ready schema migration scripts

useCases.title: Design a new Cassandra schema
useCases.description: Architect a Cassandra primary key structure for an application that needs to query by user ID and date range efficiently.

useCases.title: Optimize DynamoDB single-table design
useCases.description: Refactor a DynamoDB table to store multiple entity types (users, orders, products) in one table using composite keys.

useCases.title: Fix hot partition issues
useCases.description: Identify and resolve hot partition problems in a high-traffic DynamoDB table by improving partition key cardinality.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Cassandra Key Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me design a Cassandra primary key for a table that stores user events. I need to query by user_id and filter by event_type within a date range. What should my partition key and clustering columns be?
promptTemplates.scenario: Designing a new Cassandra table schema

promptTemplates.title: DynamoDB Single-Table Design
promptTemplates.prompt: I have three entity types: Users, Orders, and Products. Design a single DynamoDB table using composite keys where I can fetch a user's profile and all their orders in one request.

promptTemplates.title: Hot Partition Diagnosis
promptTemplates.prompt: My DynamoDB table is experiencing throttling on one partition. The partition key is 'status' (values: 'active', 'pending', 'completed'). What's wrong and how do I fix it?

promptTemplates.title: Migration from SQL to NoSQL
promptTemplates.prompt: I have a relational schema with Author and Book tables joined by author_id. How should I model this in Cassandra or DynamoDB without joins?

bestPractices: Always design partition keys with high cardinality to avoid hot partitions
Pre-compute joins at write time using denormalization instead of querying multiple tables
Use composite partition keys (e.g., USER#123#2024-01) to prevent partitions from exceeding 10GB
antiPatterns: Using low-cardinality partition keys like status='active' or gender='m' (creates hot partitions)
Using 'scatter-gather' queries that scan all partitions instead of targeting specific keys
Applying SQL modeling patterns (separate tables with foreign keys) to NoSQL databases

faq.question: What is query-first modeling?
faq.answer: Query-first modeling means designing your database schema around the specific queries your application needs, rather than trying to fit queries into an entity-based structure. List all access patterns first, then design tables to serve each pattern efficiently.

faq.question: How do I choose a partition key?
faq.answer: Choose a partition key with high cardinality (many unique values) to distribute traffic evenly across nodes. Avoid low-cardinality keys like status or gender that would create hot partitions.

faq.question: What is single-table design?
faq.answer: Single-table design stores multiple entity types in one DynamoDB table using composite partition/sort keys. This enables fetching related data (user + orders) in a single request.

faq.question: What causes hot partitions?
faq.answer: Hot partitions occur when too much traffic hits a single partition key value. This limits throughput to one node's capacity. Fix by using composite keys or sharding (e.g., USER#123#2024-01).

faq.question: Can I use joins in Cassandra or DynamoDB?
faq.answer: No. Neither Cassandra nor DynamoDB support joins. Instead, denormalize your data and store related data together, or use multiple separate tables optimized for each query pattern.

faq.question: What is the difference between GSI and LSI in DynamoDB?
faq.answer: GSI (Global Secondary Index) spans the entire table across partitions and can be created anytime. LSI (Local Secondary Index) is scoped to a single partition and must be created at table creation time.
